titleStmt.title: Unraveling the nature of quasi van der Waals Epitaxy of magne�c topological insulators Cr: (Bi x Sb 1-x ) 2 Te 3 on a GaAs (111) substrate through coherently strained interface

analytic.title: Unraveling the nature of quasi van der Waals Epitaxy of magne�c topological insulators Cr: (Bi x Sb 1-x ) 2 Te 3 on a GaAs (111) substrate through coherently strained interface

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Quasi van der Waals Epitaxy (qvdWE) has been realized for decades at the interfaces between 3D and 2D materials or van der Waals materials. The growth of magne�c topological insulators (MTI) Cr: (Bi x Sb 1- x ) 2 Te 3 (CBST) on GaAs (111) substrates for Quantum Anomalous Hall Effect (QAH) is actually one of the examples of qvdWE, which is not well no�ced despite the fact that its advantages have been used in growth of various MTI materials. This is dis�nguished from the growth of MTIs on other substrates. Although the qvdWE mode has been used in many 2D growth on III-V substrates, the specific features and mechanisms are not well demonstrated and summarized yet. Here in this work, we have for the first �me shown the features of both coherent interfaces and the existence of strain origina�ng from qvdWE at the same �me.

div.head: Main
div.p: Magne�c topological insulator (MTI) like Cr: (Bi x Sb 1-x ) 2 Te 3 , or CBST, could achieve quantum anomalous Hall (QAH) effect and spin-orbit torque (SOT) switching in the same structure. This is promising for its future applica�ons in memory or switching applica�ons with its robust surface proper�es by topological protec�on. 3 If realized, it can be more energy-efficient than tradi�onal CMOS technologies due to the extremely low stand-by energy consump�on. Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) has been demonstrated to be one of the best methods for growing CBST and other MTIs. Among all these different substrates for epitaxial growth (eg. SrTiO 3 , sapphire, InP, etc), GaAs and some other III-V substrates have stood out due to its smaller la�ce mismatch, polariza�on at interface, etc.

Recently, the specific growth mode of quasi van der Waals epitaxy (qvdWE) has been raised to describe the growth of MTIs on GaAs and some other III-V substrates. 6,7,18,19 The qvdWE mode has been applied to many different chalcogenide growths as well and has been drawing people's aten�on due to the nature that it can have coherent interface and a good crystallinity.

Unlike Van der Waals Epitaxy which has been seen on many 2D growths, in some van der Waals layers, the van der Waals force as a secondary bonding force can s�ll add some constraints to the epitaxial layer though much weaker than first order bonding. Beginning 1 decade ago the quasi van der Waals epitaxy has already been realized via graphene as an inter-layer. 1,5 Remote epitaxy also u�lizes the rela�vely stronger constraints from the 3D substrate with a 2D interlayer. 2 Similarly, the growth of van der Waals layers on a 3D substrate can also have a strong interac�on between epitaxial layer and substrates, 6,7,18,19 as in the case of CBST on GaAs (111). The specific surface poten�al and ionicity determines the nature of a quasi van der Waals growth, which has the same advantage of van der Waals epitaxy of ge�ng rid of surface roughness while at the same �me maintaining a uniform grain orienta�on as tradi�onal epitaxy.

Despite these reports, there are s�ll no clear conclusions on the root cause of qvdWE and the features of qvdWE have not been well recognized. The typical features are coherent interface and the existence of a strain within a short period of distance. While coherency is much more widely observed for qvdWE, the existence of strain is generally hard to be demonstrated due to the small thickness of strained layers in the van der Waals materials. Our goal in this work is to demonstrate both coherency and the existence of strain at the interface of epitaxial CBST on GaAs (111) substrate. X-ray pole figures and Reciprocal Space Mapping (RSM), in-situ Reflec�ve High Energy Electron Diffrac�on (RHEED), STEM (Scanning Transmission Electron Microscope) and EDS (Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy), and a Raman measurement are used to demonstrate the coherently strained interface.

To show the coherently strained interface, we have grown samples with thickness of 1 QL (quintuple layer), 2QL and 6 QL under the same condi�ons respec�vely to compare the relaxed layers and strained layers. Shown in Fig. 1. is a summary of our growth process and some basic structure characteriza�ons. during growth. The rela�ve la�ce constant is compared with ini�al value of GaAs and is calculated inversely from d-spacing from RHEED image. It shows linear transi�on at the ini�al growth from GaAs to CBST and then stabilizes during all the growth. RHEED intensity shows clear oscilla�on with a period around 51s/QL. It can be seen that the la�ce constant relaxes at around 30s which corresponds to the growth of half layer of 1QL. g) A high-angle annular dark field (HAADF) image of a 6 QL (quintuple layer) CBST cross sec�on. The Pt layer on top is deposited during the FIB milling process. The red box highlights the region where EDS is performed. h) EDS layered map shows three dis�nct regions in the field of view. The middle plot the row-averaged normalized intensity profile of three representa�ve elements, and the right plot shows the atomic percentage profile of all detected elements. i) EDS maps of 4 elements exis�ng in CBST, the atomic percentages are determined to be 62.4% (Te), 21.9% (Sb), 12.0% (Bi), and 3.7% (Cr) respec�vely.

It is no�ceable that at the ini�al growth of CBST, the la�ce constant measured from RHEED patern shows a quick and nearly linear relaxa�on from GaAs (111) to CBST, similar to the relaxa�on behavior of 3D materials 22 despite the fact that the la�ce constant relaxa�on in CBST already finished within 1 QL. The relaxa�on behavior happens within half a minute which corresponds to 3 atomic layers. The main peak from RHEED also shows no shi� through all the growth which an indica�on of 0 twisted angle. Meanwhile, the EDS have shown a uniform composi�on distribu�on through the epitaxial layers, which is also an indica�on of the coherent strained nature by ruling out the possibility of dominant composi�on evolu�on. Further inves�ga�ons by other ex-situ techniques are also done in this work as discussed below.

div.head: Coherency between epitaxial CBST and GaAs (111) substrate
div.p: We have done X-ray pole figure measurements which show almost 0-degree rela�ve twist angle between CBST and GaAs, despite the existence of a twinning. A STEM (Scanning Transmission Electron Microscope) has also demonstrated a Moiré patern that corresponds to the 0-degree twisted angle. As can be seen in Fig. 2., the pole figures show a no twist between CBST and GaAs, with error bar as the only fluctua�ons here (below 1 °). A Moiré patern by STEM has further demonstrated the coherency. Detailed analysis can be found under the figures. The Moiré patern is observed on a 1 QL CBST sample which is as thick as 6 atomic layers. It is an overall effect of both relaxed CBST la�ce constant and strained CBST or GaAs (111) la�ce constant.

Since each Moiré la�ce has hundreds of atoms here due to the 6 atomic layers, the most dominant contrast is the Moiré la�ce itself, with the crystal la�ce very faint but s�ll no�ceable here. However, the FFT (Fast Fourier Transform) has shown a much clearer signals of both GaAs and CBST. It can also be seen from the FFT patern that CBST, GaAs and Moiré la�ce are all aligned in the same direc�on, which is a further proof of the coherency. The measured twisted angle from FFT here is 0.14 ° + 0.08 °, which all comes from the error bar.

div.head: Existence of Strain in the initially growth of CBST on a GaAs (111) substrate
div.p: Reciprocal Space Mapping (RSM) has been a useful tool for strain analysis. 22 Shown in Supplementary Informa�on is the RSM results from a lab-built X-ray diffrac�on machine. For vdW materials it is difficult to see strain. Here for the first �me, we have demonstrated the strain by RSM at CHESS as shown in Fig. 3.

We have done the RSM on both Te-annealed GaAs (111) substrate and the 1 QL, 2 QL and 6 QL CBST samples respec�vely. The CBST samples all show a consistent result on the strained layer, which is indicated by the faint peak across the GaAs peak. The strained layer shows up at the same in-plane la�ce constant of GaAs, which is a clear proof of the existence of strain.

It can be seen that a consistent relaxed CBST layer also shows up at the right side of the GaAs or strained CBST. As the thickness increases, the relaxed layers shows a stronger peak and a clearer thickness fringes, which can be used to dis�nguish the relaxed and the strained layers within CBST.

It is also interes�ng to note that the sharp streaky peaks of CBST all shows clear fringes despite they are only a few nm s thick, which is a sign that the films are very smooth benefited by the qvdWE mode. We also also done Raman measurements which shows shi�ing of peaks and width broadening in Fig. 4. , another sign marching the existence of strain. Peak posi�ons are summarized in Table 1. There has already been some reports of showing the substrate effect of van der Waals or 2D materials by Raman which use either the E g mode that is sensi�ve to strain or the S mode which can be used to demonstrate the in-plane la�ce strain specifically. Here we have shown the effect in both modes that corresponds to the in-plane compressive strain.
It is also no�ceable that the thinner layers have a broader peak, which is another signature of the overall peak shi�ing.
With these results, this is the 1 st �me demonstra�on of the strain features of qvdWE.

div.head: Discussion
div.p: In conclusion, we have shown the coherency by 2 signs: 1) X-ray pole figures and 2) a Moiré patern by STEM. We have also shown the coherent strain by 3 signs: 1) in-situ RHEED, 2) RSM by a high energy X-ray beam and 3) Raman measurements. These are the first �me demonstra�on of a dominant coherency and strain at the same �me in qvdWE modes.

It is important for the effect on the quan�za�on window which is a following up study of this work. This can be used to further demonstrate the materials tuning of the sample quality through growth. The disappear of the strain at grain boundary due to the coherency might be another factor that influencing the composi�on in quan�zed samples of MTI besides the doping and substrate band bending effect.

div.head: MBE growth
div.p: The samples are grown by a Perkin-Elmer Molecular Beam Epitaxy system. All the cells used for CBST growth in this work are standard Knudsen effusion cells. Commercial GaAs (111)B substrate is loaded into chamber by Indium moun�ng at the backside to assist uniform temperature distribu�on. The substrate is heated up to and kept at 600 ℃ inside the growth chamber under a high Te pressure (T Te = 380 ℃) to remove surface oxide u�lizing the surface dissocia�on of GaAs. The high Te pressure helps reduce the over-annealing of GaAs substrates. Substrates are immediately cooled down to a growth temperature of T sub = 210 ℃ in Te pressure a�er the oxide is removed. The samples are grown at the condi�on of T Cr = 1090 ℃, T Bi =462 ℃, T Sb = 300 ℃, and T Te = 330 ℃. In-situ RHEED is used to monitor the numbers of quintuple layers grown by RHEED intensity oscilla�on. Typical samples are grown into 6 QL. A�er growth, samples are cooled down in Te pressure. In this work, we have grown samples at 1 QL, 2 QL and 6 QL respec�vely using the same growth condi�on above to demonstrate the strain effect and interfacial proper�es.

div.head: STEM imaging and EDS analysis
div.p: The electron microscopy data is acquired in a FEI Titan 80-300 STEM without an aberra�on corrector or a monochromator. The energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) data is acquired with an Oxford X-MaxN 100TLE 100 mm 2 silicon dri� detector (SDD). The imaging condi�ons have an accelera�ng voltage of 300 kV, 10 mrad convergence semi-angle, and 0.18 nA beam current. The CBST cross sec�on is prepared with gallium focused ion beam (FIB) in a Nova 600 SEM/FIB System. A layer of pla�num is deposited with a small FIB current to minimize damage to the top surface. The EDS map has a live �me of 10 mins, and the atomic percentages of detected elements are determined with Cliff-Lorimer method.

div.head: X-ray diffraction, Pole Figures and Reciprocal Space Mapping (RSM)
div.p: The ω-2θ scan is done by a standard Panaly�cal powder-compa�ble X-ray Diffrac�on machine with a step size of 0.1 ° and a range 10 °-70 ° which include key peaks from CBST and GaAs.
Pole figures and a RSM at a specific angle are measured by a home-built X-ray diffrac�on equipment. We used TAD A2 A2 and glancing incidence for the CBST epilayer.

We have also done a 3D RSM at Cornell High Energy Electron Source (CHESS) for CBST grown on GaAs (111) substrates. All of these maps were collected at photon energy of 15 keV, nominal temperature of 100 K from a nitrogen cryostream, collected using the rota�on diffrac�on method. The images were collected with a Pilatus 6M detector at the the QM2 beam line at Cornell High Energy Synchrotron Source.

div.head: Raman Spectroscopy
div.p: Raman measurements are done using a home-built cryogenic polarized precision Raman system. The measurements are done with a vacuum level of 1 x 10 -7 hpa, an excita�on laser 532.16 nm (2.33 eV), a laser power 400 μW and a spectrometer window size around 20 nm (around 750 cm -1 near 532 nm).

figure.head: Fig. 1 .
figure.figDesc: Fig.1. Growth process of CBST and basic structure characteriza�ons. a)-b) In-situ Reflec�ve High-Energy Electron Diffrac�on (RHEED) patern of GaAs (111) substrate (a)) and CBST (b)). The sample is showing <1 1 -2 0> direc�on in the RHEED image and is fixed at the same loca�on during growth without rota�on. c) X-ray diffrac�on of the grown CBST sample in comparison to bare substrate in ω-2θ scan. It shows clear peaks corresponding to CBST besides the peaks from GaAs substrate. d) Recording of d-spacing image during growth. e)-f) Time evolu�on of rela�ve la�ce constant value (e)) and RHEED intensity (f)) during growth. The rela�ve la�ce constant is compared with ini�al value of GaAs and is calculated inversely from d-spacing from RHEED image. It shows linear transi�on at the ini�al growth from GaAs to CBST and then stabilizes during all the growth. RHEED intensity shows clear oscilla�on with a period around 51s/QL. It can be seen that the la�ce constant relaxes at around 30s which corresponds to the growth of half layer of 1QL. g) A high-angle annular dark field (HAADF) image of a 6 QL (quintuple layer) CBST cross sec�on. The Pt layer on top is deposited during the FIB milling process. The red box highlights the region where EDS is performed. h) EDS layered map shows three dis�nct regions in the field of view.The middle plot the row-averaged normalized intensity profile of three representa�ve elements, and the right plot shows the atomic percentage profile of all detected elements. i) EDS maps of 4 elements exis�ng in CBST, the atomic percentages are determined to be 62.4% (Te), 21.9% (Sb), 12.0% (Bi), and 3.7% (Cr) respec�vely.

figure.head: Fig. 2 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 2. Pole Figures from X-ray Reciprocal Space Mapping (RSM) and Moiré patern from Scanning Transmission Electron Microscope (STEM) show uniform in-plane orienta�on of CBST on GaAs (111) with no global intrinsic twisted angle observed. a)-b) Pole figures of CBST (1 0 -1 10) plane (a)) and GaAs (3 3 1) plane (b)). The two scans are made out of the same alignment and show 0 degree twisted angle. It is also no�ceable that for each peak on the pole figure of CBST, there is only one dominant peak observed, which is a sign of uniform in-plane orienta�on of CBST grown on GaAs (111) substrates. Despite the twinning observed, the sample shows good crystallinity quality. c) Moiré patern observed by STEM imaging. The Moiré patern shows a hexagonal symmetry system, with the local contrast patern showing regular triangle rela�onship. The Moiré la�ce is measured to be around 2.9-3.0 nm based on the figure. d) A FFT of Moiré patern observed. e) A Moiré patern model (green: substrate or strained epilayer; red/yellow: relaxed epilayer)based on 0 degree twisted angle and the la�ces constants of GaAs (111) and CBST (0001). The la�ce constant used in the model is 4.35 Å and is calculated based on the composi�on from EDS measurement Cr: Bi: Sb: Te = 3.7%: 12.0%: 21.9%: 62.4%. Te site from epi-layer and As-corresponding site from substrate layer are set overlapping at the 0-point based on the epitaxial

figure.head: Fig. 3 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 3. Reciprocal Space Mapping (RSM) of a) GaAs (111) substrate a�er Te annealing; b) 1 QL CBST grown on GaAs (111); c) 2 QL CBST grown on GaAs (111); d) 6 QL CBST grown on GaAs (111).The white square marks the peak from GaAs bulk substrates in each sample. The blue arrow shows the peaks which has the same in-plane la�ce constant as GaAs in samples only with CBST, indica�ng the existence of a coherently strained CBST at the ini�al quasi van der Waals Epitaxy growth mode. The strained layer shows up at a same loca�on (same in-plane la�ce constant as GaAs) in samples with different thickness. The relaxed layers are also marked in black arrows in each samples. It can be seen that the relaxed layers show different fringes and intensity due to the difference in their thickness, which is dis�nguished from the coherently strained layers.

figure.head: Fig. 4 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 4. Raman Spectroscopy on CBST with 1 QL, 2 QL and 6 QL.

rela�onship that Te atom from CBST botom layer prefer to sit on top of Te atom formed from subs�tu�on of As on GaAs substrate. The Moiré patern shows Moiré la�ce parallel to those of the crystal la�ce, which is the typical feature of a 0 twisted angle. The Moiré la�ce length is 12 �mes (21 � 1 � ) spacing of GaAs here, which is about 28 Å, matching the measured Moiré la�ce length in c).Theore�cal Moiré la�ce constant calcula�on: GaAs cubic la�ce constant is 5.75 Å, (21� 1 � ) spacing is 5.75/√2 2 + 1 2 + 1 2 ≈ 2.35 Å. 2.35 * 12 = 28.2 Å.Measured Moiré la�ce constant calcula�on: k-space first order ring radius is 0.0382 Å^-1. Assuming a perfect triangular Moiré la�ce, then the la�ce constant is 1/0.0382/(√3/2) ≈ 30.2 Å.

figure.head: Table 1 . Raman measurement results on CBST of 1 QL, 2 QL and 6 QL grown on GaAs

div.head: Acknowledgement
div.p: The authors would like to thank Shuyu Cheng, Qing Lou, Xiyue Zhang, Peng Zhang and Ziming Shao for useful discussions.

div.head: Author Contribu�ons
div.p: Yuxing Ren
